architecture_fit.note: Decodes raw integer token columns and returns (A-B)^2; no attention, residual stream, embedding, or superposition — it bypasses the goal's actual question rather than answering it.

baseline_comparison.note: Framework-computed linear baseline (0.76 canonical) is beaten by the model (1.0), but the gap is hollow since the model cheats by reading exact token integers, and no strawman was designed by the attempt.

faithfulness.note: No model, ablation, or causal evidence that any attention mechanism uses XOR; README only notes that flipping '-' to '+' breaks the formula and proposes no causal check.

operating_range.note: Perfect 1.0 accuracy across the full p∈[0.1,0.9] sweep, but trivially so via exact decode; the goal's real scaling axis (superposition cosine) is never probed.

hardcoded_weights_bonus.note: Fully hand-coded, but it is an arithmetic token decode, not a hand-set attention/superposition circuit, so it does not demonstrate the target mechanism.

visual_judgement.note: app.py fails the boot-check: 'from task import _fmt_p, _check_unit' raises ImportError (those live in benchmark.py); demo cannot launch, and the truth-table append logic is buggy.

visualisation_rationale.note: README has a coherent 'Why this visualisation' section, but the truth-table viz is trivial and disconnected from the goal's superposition/attention axis.

notes: The attempt achieves perfect XOR accuracy (robustness 1.0) but does so by hand-decoding the raw integer token columns and returning (A-B)^2 — it uses no attention, no transformer block, and no superposition, so it never engages the goal's question of whether an attention head can express XOR. There is no model, no causal/ablation evidence, and the linear-baseline lift is framework-supplied rather than a designed comparison. The Gradio app additionally fails its boot-check (ImportError on _fmt_p/_check_unit), so the demo cannot even launch; worst-of architecture/baseline/faithfulness lands this at fail.
